history: Causal Loop Diagram (8-10 variables with loops and delays) → Customer service representatives (high routine tasks, clear automation trajectory) → Focus on systemic feedback (AI capability, adoption rate, worker displacement, political pressure, regulation) as self-reinforcing cycle
anand_notes: Clearly doable; PROBABLY TOO EASY FOR 7 DAYS
consultant_v2_notes: The CLD approach is feasible and well-structured with genuine feedback richness—AI capability, adoption pressure, displacement, and regulatory backlash form a credible reinforcing cycle. However, customer service is a narrow, deterministic domain where automation outcomes are already well-understood; the plan risks being too straightforward for a week-long assignment. The systemic loops are sound, but there's limited room for surprising emergent insights or meaningful scenario contrast—'high support' in customer service typically just delays the inevitable. Consider broadening to a role with more heterogeneous skill pathways (junior developers, data analysts, technical writers) where worker responses genuinely diverge.

history: Causal Loop Diagram (8-10 variables with loops and delays) → Software developers (moderate automation pressure, skill-shift dynamics) → Broad focus: AI disrupting multiple dev roles (juniors, QA, DevOps, technical leads through different pressures)
anand_notes: SAME AS ABOVE; COULD BE SOMEWHAT MORE COMPLEX THAN CALL CENTER
consultant_v2_notes: The domain choice and multi-role framing are strong—software developers offer genuine systemic richness across junior/QA/DevOps/lead stratification. However, CLD risks becoming too broad and vague without anchoring on a single focal mechanism; 'skill-shift dynamics' must be the explicit emergent story (e.g., does junior demand collapse while senior demand rises?), not just a backdrop. The 8-10 variable constraint is tight for this scope—you'll need to ruthlessly prune to avoid a tangled diagram that obscures the core loops. Feasibility is solid (CLD always is), but scope fit depends entirely on whether you pick ONE clear feedback story to tell rather than trying to model all four roles equally.

history: Agent-Based Simulation (15-20 workers with states and emergence) → Software developers (mixed routine/creative work, visible skill stratification) → Use NetLogo (visual, built-in UI, easier scenario comparison, exports to screenshot)
anand_notes: DOABLE & ALSO APPROPRIATE SCOPE FOR A WEEK
consultant_v2_notes: Excellent tool choice and domain selection—NetLogo is the right fit, software developers offer genuinely stratifiable skill dynamics, and 15-20 agents is perfectly scoped. The main quality risk is ensuring skill stratification emerges from interaction (task assignment, learning, burnout feedback) rather than being hard-coded as initial trait variation; the plan needs explicit clarity on what mechanism generates emergence vs. what is programmed setup.

history: Agent-Based Simulation (15-20 workers with states and emergence) → Software developers (mixed routine/creative work, visible skill stratification) → Use a spreadsheet-based simulation (Excel/Sheets with VBA or formulas, fastest prototyping, limited emergence complexity)
anand_notes: EXCEL/VBA MAY NOT BE THE RIGHT TOOL; LOWER SCOPE FIT AS SOMEONE COULD SPEND MORE TIME TO GET EVERYTHING WORKING
consultant_v2_notes: Spreadsheet-based ABM is the wrong tool for this assignment. Excel/VBA will consume 2-3 days in setup and debugging friction, leaving insufficient time for scenario comparison and interpretation quality. True emergence (skill stratification dynamics, genuine agent heterogeneity) is nearly impossible to implement and validate in a spreadsheet; you'll end up hard-coding outcomes instead of discovering them. NetLogo or Python would deliver a far stronger model in the same calendar time.
